items.q: Why did British women get the vote in 1918?
items.correct: A combination of pre-war suffragist and suffragette campaigning, women's contribution to WWI, and broader political changes including extending male suffrage.
items.misconception: Women got the vote as a reward for their work in WWI.
items.random_wrong: Emmeline Pankhurst was elected to Parliament and voted it through.
items.why: The 'WWI reward' story is popular but only partial — older property-owning women got the vote, not the young factory workers. Pre-war campaigning and political pragmatism mattered as much.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: Why was Vesalius significant in the history of medicine?
items.correct: His 1543 De Humani Corporis Fabrica corrected Galen's errors via human dissection and accurate illustration, founding modern anatomy though not curing disease.
items.misconception: Vesalius discovered the cause of disease and ended humoral theory.
items.why: Vesalius improved anatomy but disease theory still ran on humours for centuries. Students conflate progress in one area (structure) with progress in another (cause of illness).

items.q: Who led reformist Czechoslovakia during Prague Spring?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dubcek. “Tito” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: When was the Berlin Wall built?
items.why: It's 1961. “1949” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
items.why: Exact answer: 1989 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
